front: The risk response strategies at a glance
back:
Five strategies for threats and five mirrored ones for opportunities. Escalate and Accept appear on both sides.

- Threats: Escalate, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept
- Opportunities: Escalate, Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 196' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='85' y='22' fill='#B5462F' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace'>THREATS</text><text x='238' y='22' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' font-family='monospace'>OPPORTUNITIES</text><line x1='18' y1='32' x2='302' y2='32' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='160' y1='14' x2='160' y2='186' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='85' y='58' fill='currentColor' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Escalate</text><text x='238' y='58' fill='currentColor' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Escalate</text><text x='85' y='86' fill='#B5462F' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Avoid</text><text x='238' y='86' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Exploit</text><text x='85' y='114' fill='#B5462F' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Transfer</text><text x='238' y='114' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Share</text><text x='85' y='142' fill='#B5462F' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Mitigate</text><text x='238' y='142' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Enhance</text><text x='85' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Accept</text><text x='238' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='12.5' text-anchor='middle'>Accept</text></svg>
figure.caption: Threats vs opportunities. Escalate and Accept are shared.

tip: Each opportunity strategy mirrors a threat one: Avoid↔Exploit, Transfer↔Share, Mitigate↔Enhance.
source: PMBOK Guide — Plan Risk Responses (stable across 6th–8th ed.)

back: The threat is outside the project's authority or scope (e.g. organisational or programme level). Hand it to the right owner; once escalated, it's no longer monitored on the project risk register.
tip: Escalate when responding is genuinely beyond your authority — not just because it's hard.

back: Eliminate the threat entirely by removing its cause — e.g. change the plan, cut the risky scope, or extend the schedule. Strongest option for high-probability, high-impact threats when feasible.
tip: Avoid removes the threat; mitigate only shrinks it.

back: Shift the impact and ownership of the threat to a third party — insurance, warranties, performance bonds, or outsourcing. The risk still exists; someone else now carries it (usually for a fee).
tip: Transfer almost always costs a risk premium. It shifts impact, it doesn't remove the risk.

back: Reduce the probability and/or impact to an acceptable level — prototyping, more testing, redundancy, choosing a more stable supplier. The most common response when avoid/transfer aren't practical.
tip: Mitigation is proactive reduction; it's often paired with a contingency reserve.

back:
Take no proactive action, usually because other responses aren't cost-effective.

- Active acceptance: set up a contingency reserve / fallback plan
- Passive acceptance: simply document and monitor
tip: Acceptance is a deliberate choice, not ignoring the risk. Low-priority risks are typical candidates.

back: The opportunity is bigger than the project — it benefits the programme or organisation more than this project. Hand it to the level that can actually pursue it.
tip: Escalate (both sides) means 'not ours to own' — pass it to the right authority.

back: Make the opportunity certain to happen — assign your best people, add resources, or change the plan to capture it. The strongest opportunity response (mirrors Avoid).
tip: Exploit = make it definitely happen; Enhance only improves the odds.

back: Partner with a third party better able to capture the opportunity — a joint venture, teaming agreement, or partnership. You capture part of an opportunity you couldn't fully realise alone (mirrors Transfer).
tip: Share an opportunity the way you'd transfer a threat — bring in someone better placed.

back: Increase the probability and/or positive impact of the opportunity — e.g. add resources early to finish ahead of schedule. The opportunity twin of Mitigate.
tip: Enhance improves the odds/size; Exploit locks it in.

back: Don't actively pursue it, but take advantage if it arises. Used when the opportunity isn't worth dedicated effort.
tip: Same idea as accepting a threat — be ready, but don't spend to chase it.

front: How do you choose a strategy for a high-probability, high-impact threat?
back:
Work down in rough order of strength, weighing cost and feasibility:

- Avoid (remove the cause) if you can
- else Transfer the impact
- else Mitigate to an acceptable level
- else Accept with a contingency reserve

Escalate at any point if it's beyond your authority.
tip: Match the response to the risk's priority and your appetite — not every risk needs an active response.
